summary: Gruber responds to David Simon's argument that major newspapers like the NYT and Washington Post should erect pay walls to save their business. While acknowledging the real crisis — newspapers are hemorrhaging money as readers shift from print to web — Gruber argues pay walls would make things worse by driving away readers and reducing ad revenue without generating enough subscription income to compensate. He dismantles Simon's analogy to cable TV, noting that cable offered dramatically improved service in exchange for payment, while pay walls would charge for the same free content. Gruber concludes that the real problem isn't revenue but the bloated operational structure of newspapers, and that lean online-only publications point the way forward.
key_points: Pay walls would cause newspapers to lose most readers, reducing ad revenue by more than subscription fees could offset, making the financial situation worse
Consumer psychology treats web content as inherently free — newsstand pricing works because physical copies have tangible production costs, but web pages are 'just ones and zeroes'
The cable TV analogy fails because cable offered vastly improved service (more channels, better quality) for a fee, while pay walls would charge for the same product readers already get free
Newspapers' real problem is operational bloat, not insufficient revenue — lean online-only publications are already profitable
The Wall Street Journal's success with pay walls is the exception, serving a niche market accustomed to paying for immediate financial information
money_quotes: What feels like a fair price for a copy of a web page, on the other hand, is nothing. They're just ones and zeroes.
Newspapers will never make as much money from online advertising as they did from print because they will never again exert so much control over the supply of news.
The primary problem with newspaper companies isn't their revenue. It's the size and scope of their operations.

Simon, along with everyone else who thinks online subscription fees can save the newspaper industry, is effectively arguing that the world will change to support newspapers. The truth is that newspapers must change to adapt to the world.
Just because the extinction of newspapers would be a tragic loss doesn't mean it won't happen.
With newspaper web access, Simon is proposing that readers start paying for the same thing they now get for free.
Classified ads in particular were a source of obscene profits for daily newspapers. They've lost the market to Craigslist, which does a better job and lists most of them for free.
key_insight: Newspapers' existential crisis stems not from insufficient reader willingness to pay but from bloated legacy operations that cannot compete with lean digital-native publications, and pay walls would only accelerate the decline by sacrificing audience without solving the structural problem.
